docs.text: Bob's Universal and Very Intelligent System ¶ This is the documentation for my home Kubernetes clusters in a declarative state. Flux watches my clusters repository and makes the changes to them based on the YAML manifests. A lot of inspiration for my cluster came from the people that have shared their clusters over at awesome-home-kubernetes . Another important component of BUVIS as a system are my dotfiles, scripts and other artifacts . A lot of inspiration for my cluster came from the people that have shared their clusters over at awesome-home-kubernetes . Another important component of BUVIS as a system are my dotfiles, scripts and other artifacts .

docs.text: teedy: I couldn't upload documents to it papermerge: slow and returning 500 often paperless: can't consume all the documents, so my documents storage must be split wireguard: I couldn't get it work on Proxmox VMs deluge: web interface was loosing connection and I couldn't add ubuntu torrent from file
docs.title: Abandoned

docs.text: Preparation ¶ Tools ¶ Install the following tools on your workstation: pre-commit direnv gnupg and sops ansible kubectl krew minio kubectl plugin Services ¶ Create accounts: ZeroSSL to get certificates for ingress Slack to get notifications from Flux Gitops ¶ Add renovate to Github Make sure that postgres database kubernetes doesn't exist on datastore server Update EAB Credentials for ZeroSSL ACME Generate new key-secret pair at ZeroSSL | Developer Update secrets for cert-manager (do this in all cluster directories): sops operations/kube-system/cert-manager/secrets.yaml Update keyID in operations/kube-system/cert-manager/operators/zerossl.yaml (do this in all cluster directories) Export GITHUB_TOKEN environment variable into .envrc files with GitHub personal access token generated specifically for Flux Export SLACK_WEBHOOK_URL environment variable into .envrc files, get incoming webhook address <SLACK_WEBHOOK_URL> from Slack Enable SOPS for Flux (do only once in a lifetime) Generate GPG key with no password protection. You can't protect the key with password, because Flux has no way of entering it when decrypting the secrets. Get fingerprint of the key <SOPS_KEY_FINGERPRINT> gpg --list-secret-keys Export SOPS_KEY_FINGERPRINT environment variable into .envrc files with the value from previous step Network ¶ SSH to home router Assign IP addresses to nodes and VMs in /etc/dhcpd.conf , push router's IP as DNS to them Install bgpd on home router Copy network/router/etc/bgpd.conf to home router's /etc to configure bgpd to peer with the cluster Production cluster ¶ Anything that follows supposes you are working in production directory . Install Proxmox ¶ Download Proxmox installation iso Burn it to USB stick . Note: in macOS, use 1M instead of 1m as block size Boot Proxmox machine from the USB stick Unauthorized change message will display, confirm it Set the correct date and time in BIOS Disable onboard wifi and bluetooth Check that disks are recognized Boot - Secure Boot = Disabled Exit, save, reboot Install Proxmox following the installation wizard (use router's IP as DNS server) Enter root's password to PM_PASS environment variable in .envrc Check that you can connect to Proxmox management UI at https://<server_ip>:8006 Remove subscription notice SSH to proxmox server Go to UI site source: cd /usr/share/javascript/proxmox-widget-toolkit/ Backup the file you'll modify: cp proxmoxlib.js proxmoxlib.js.bak Edit proxmoxlib.js : vi proxmoxlib.js Find Ext.Msg.show({ title: gettext('No valid subscription'), Replace with void({ title: gettext('No valid subscription'), Restart Proxmox UI: systemctl restart pveproxy.service Clear browser cache and reconnect UI Use community repo Edit sources: vi /etc/apt/sources.list Add deb http://download.proxmox.com/debian bullseye pve-no-subscription Disable enterprise repo Go to apt sources directory: cd /etc/apt/sources.list.d Backup enterprise list: cp pve-enterprise.list pve-enterprise.list.bak Edit enterprise list: vi pve-enterprise.list Comment out this line: deb https://enterprise.proxmox.com/debian/pve bullseye pve-enterprise Update the system: apt update && apt dist-upgrade Create storage for Persistent Volumes: <NODENAME> - Disks - ZFS , create <NODENAME>-tank on entire /dev/sda Install temperature sensors reading tools: apt install xsensors (then use sensors to read temperature measurements) Fix Proxmox on Asus PN50 ¶ I faced the following issues in spring 2022 when installing Proxmox to Asus PN50. In May 2022, I installed Proxmox to Asus PN51 and there were no issues with that. Network will be down on the first boot. Restart it: systemctl restart networking Reboot gets stuck when exiting KVM. Upgrade to kernel 5.15: apt install pve-kernel-5.15 pve-kernel-5.15.5-1-pve pve-headers-5.15 pve-headers-5.15.5-1-pve References: Realtek 8125 issues | Proxmox forum Shutdown hangs on kvm exiting | Proxmox forum Create VM template ¶ SSH to proxmox machine Get the image for VM: wget https://cloud-images.ubuntu.com/focal/current/focal-server-cloudimg-amd64.img . Note: I couldn't use the following images: Arch Linux: it lacks overlay module, so k3s won't run CentOS: all VMs are named localhost, so Kubernetes will add one node only Create VM: qm create 9000 --name "ubuntu-cloudimg" --memory 4096 --cpu cputype=host --cores 4 --serial0 socket --vga serial0 --net0 virtio,bridge=vmbr0,tag=20 --agent enabled=1,fstrim_cloned_disks=1 Import the image to local storage: qm importdisk 9000 focal-server-cloudimg-amd64.img local-lvm --format qcow2 Attach the disk to VM: qm set 9000 --scsihw virtio-scsi-pci --scsi0 local-lvm:vm-9000-disk-0 Add cloudinit CDROM drive: qm set 9000 --ide2 local-lvm:cloudinit Set disk to boot: qm set 9000 --boot c --bootdisk scsi0 Convert VM to template: qm template 9000 Repeat steps 2-8 on every node in Proxmox cluster. ID 9000 must be incremented as this ID must be unique inside the cluster. Bootstrap ¶ Set workstation's DNS to 1.1.1.1, because Blocky isn't running Run make install in cluster's directory. Staging cluster ¶ Anything that follows supposes you are working in staging directory . Bootstrap the cluster ¶ Flash the nodes: ansible-playbook infrastructure/ansible/flash-node Get kube config: ansible-playbook infrastructure/ansible/get-cluster-config Limit workloads scheduling to master nodes: kubectl taint node <master> node-role.kubernetes.io/master:NoSchedule ` CNI deployment ¶ Install bgpd on home router Copy infrastructure/etc/bgpd.conf to home router's /etc to configure bgpd to peer with the cluster Install Calico operator (manifest from Calico multi-node install modified to refer to arm64 images): kubectl apply -f infrastructure/manifests/deploy-tigera-operator.yaml ` Install Calico (manifest modified to use BGP peering with home router): kubectl apply -f infrastructure/manifests/install-calico.yaml ` Workloads deployment ¶ Flux watches my clusters repository and makes the changes to them based on the YAML manifests. To install Flux, run make flux Persistent volumes migration ¶ Longhorn connects to NAS backup directory and the backups aren't linked to the previous installation, so they can be used in the new cluster without any problems. Connect to Longhorn frontend Go to Backups Restore all volumes from backup The Longhorn volumes and PVs have a static name, so the workloads will pick the volumes restored from backup if you keep the name (check Use Previous Name checkbox in Restore Backup dialog)

docs.text: Flux watches my clusters repository and makes the changes to them based on the YAML manifests. To install Flux, run make flux
docs.title: Workloads deployment

docs.text: Longhorn connects to NAS backup directory and the backups aren't linked to the previous installation, so they can be used in the new cluster without any problems. Connect to Longhorn frontend Go to Backups Restore all volumes from backup The Longhorn volumes and PVs have a static name, so the workloads will pick the volumes restored from backup if you keep the name (check Use Previous Name checkbox in Restore Backup dialog)
docs.title: Persistent volumes migration

docs.text: Upgrade ¶ Flux ¶ This is automated using Github Action . Nodes ¶ Determine latest k3s version at k3s release channels Update the version in ansible group vars - k3s_version Run buvis-upgrade provided by buvis dotfiles Recreate ¶ Backup ¶ Go to Longhorn UI and take note of volume names and size which you want to restore into recreated cluster Check that daily backup is available for volumes from step 1 Perform application specific backup for the applications considered critical home-assistant linkace monica Scale down the deployments with fresh data and trigger a backup Destroy ¶ Run make destroy in cluster's directory. Bootstrap ¶ Set workstation's DNS to 1.1.1.1, because Blocky isn't running Run make install in cluster's directory. Usage ¶ Use secret values in manifests directly ¶ Define key-value pair in cluster-secrets sops flux-system/extras/cluster-secrets.yaml Refer to the secret value in manifest file password : ${SECRET_PASSWORD} Use secret values in manifests from a file ¶ Create secret.yaml --- apiVersion : v1 kind : Secret metadata : name : oauth2-proxy-secrets type : Opaque stringData : clientID : "<secret_value>" clientSecret : "<secret_value>" Include secrets into manifest clientID : valueFrom : secretKeyRef : name : oauth2-proxy-secrets key : clientID Encrypt the secrets before pushing (TODO: use pre-commit hook) sops --encrypt --in-place secret.yaml Wait for another helm release (dependency) ¶ Some releases depend on others. You may get errors when reconciling such releases too early. Flux will reconcile something only once something else it depends on is available when dependsOn is mentionned in helm release manifest. It can refer to a name of another helm release across all namespaces. See Flux documentation Example ¶ --- apiVersion : helm.toolkit.fluxcd.io/v2beta1 kind : HelmRelease metadata : name : longhorn namespace : longhorn-system spec : dependsOn : - name : ingress-nginx namespace : kube-system - name : oauth2-proxy namespace : kube-system - name : cert-manager namespace : kube-system Troubleshooting ¶ Flux can't reconcile a helmrelease ¶ Get status of all helmreleases flux get helmreleases --all-namespaces or shorter flux get hr -A Make sure that sources can be accessed (as there might be typos) flux get sources helm -A flux get sources git -A flux get sources chart -A Get more information on helm release kubectl describe helmrelease <RELEASE_NAME> -n <RELEASE_NAMESPACE> Restart when Flux gave up flux resume hr <RELEASE_NAME> -n <RELEASE_NAMESPACE> Get CPU temperature in Proxmox for Asus PN50 ¶ Look at k10temp-pci-00c3 in the output of sensors command.
docs.title: Operations

docs.text: This is automated using Github Action .

docs.text: Create secret.yaml --- apiVersion : v1 kind : Secret metadata : name : oauth2-proxy-secrets type : Opaque stringData : clientID : "<secret_value>" clientSecret : "<secret_value>" Include secrets into manifest clientID : valueFrom : secretKeyRef : name : oauth2-proxy-secrets key : clientID Encrypt the secrets before pushing (TODO: use pre-commit hook) sops --encrypt --in-place secret.yaml
docs.title: Use secret values in manifests from a file

docs.text: Some releases depend on others. You may get errors when reconciling such releases too early. Flux will reconcile something only once something else it depends on is available when dependsOn is mentionned in helm release manifest. It can refer to a name of another helm release across all namespaces. See Flux documentation
docs.title: Wait for another helm release (dependency)

docs.text: --- apiVersion : helm.toolkit.fluxcd.io/v2beta1 kind : HelmRelease metadata : name : longhorn namespace : longhorn-system spec : dependsOn : - name : ingress-nginx namespace : kube-system - name : oauth2-proxy namespace : kube-system - name : cert-manager namespace : kube-system
docs.title: Example

docs.text: Get status of all helmreleases flux get helmreleases --all-namespaces or shorter flux get hr -A Make sure that sources can be accessed (as there might be typos) flux get sources helm -A flux get sources git -A flux get sources chart -A Get more information on helm release kubectl describe helmrelease <RELEASE_NAME> -n <RELEASE_NAMESPACE> Restart when Flux gave up flux resume hr <RELEASE_NAME> -n <RELEASE_NAMESPACE>

docs.text: This is the core of gitops. To edit cluster-secrets.yaml , run: sops cluster-secrets.yaml
docs.title: flux-system

docs.text: This hosts the application for personal efficiency. Baikal ¶ Prepare ¶ Baikal docker image won't create all directories it needs. You have to do that upfront. Reference: https://github.com/ckulka/baikal-docker/issues/29 SSH to NFS server Create the directories: mkdir -p {/mnt/tank/pv/baikal/data/db,/mnt/tank/pv/baikal/config} Change the access rights: chmod -R 775 /mnt/tank/pv/baikal Change the ownership: chown -R 101:101 /mnt/tank/pv/baikal/* Client setup ¶ iPhone ¶ Settings - Calendar - Accounts - Add Account Server = https://cal.buvis.net/cal.php/principals/<USER> Enter credentials for the Rename the account to baikal BusyCal ¶ Preferences - Accounts - Server address = https://cal.buvis.net/dav.php/ Enter credentials the and it will fetch its calendars Outlook ¶ Click Calendar icon at the bottome Right click a calendar group - Add Calendar - From Internet Location = https://cal.buvis.net/cal.php/calendars/<USER>/<CALENDAR>?export When asked for credentials, enter the username as \<USER> to avoid login with default domain Linkace ¶ Setup ¶ Comment out volumeMounts in deployment.yaml (otherwise /app/.env will be readonly and step 5 will fail) Deploy by pushing to repository Exec into pod: kubectl exec -it deployment/linkace -n gtd -- sh Fix /app/.env permissions: chmod 666 /app/.env Navigate to Linkace on buvis and complete the setup Database address and password can be found in linkace-env ConfigMap Create the user account Get app key from pod's /app/.env and copy it to cluster-secrets SECRET_LINKACE_APP_KEY Uncomment volumeMounts in deployment.yaml Push back to repo Reference: https://www.linkace.org/docs/v1/setup/setup-with-docker/simple/ Import bookmarks ¶ Exec into pod with UTF-8 support: LANG=en_US.UTF-8 kubectl exec -it deployment/linkace -n gtd -- sh Start creating storage/bookmarks.html from heredoc: cat << EOF > storage/bookmarks.html Paste content from a backup linkace-export.html file End heredoc by typing EOF on its own line Import to linkace: ./artisan links:import bookmarks.html --skip-check --skip-meta-generation Lists are not captured in html export. You'll need to recreate them from csv backup. Reference: - https://github.com/Kovah/LinkAce/issues/287#issuecomment-860229837 - https://www.linkace.org/docs/v1/cli/#import-links-from-a-html-bookmarks-file Monica ¶ Run Sequel Pro Connect to monica-mariadb service using its Cluster IP and monica-mariadb secret Force delete all tables Create the tables by running php artisan migrate in monica pod File - Import sql backup
docs.title: gtd

docs.text: Exec into pod with UTF-8 support: LANG=en_US.UTF-8 kubectl exec -it deployment/linkace -n gtd -- sh Start creating storage/bookmarks.html from heredoc: cat << EOF > storage/bookmarks.html Paste content from a backup linkace-export.html file End heredoc by typing EOF on its own line Import to linkace: ./artisan links:import bookmarks.html --skip-check --skip-meta-generation Lists are not captured in html export. You'll need to recreate them from csv backup. Reference: - https://github.com/Kovah/LinkAce/issues/287#issuecomment-860229837 - https://www.linkace.org/docs/v1/cli/#import-links-from-a-html-bookmarks-file

docs.title: Monica

docs.text: This provides the cluster homepage with links to all services.
docs.title: hajimari

docs.text: This is the cluster's system namespace.
docs.title: kube-system

docs.text: This provides storage to the cluster. I'm using Rancher's Longhorn . See manifests . Setup ¶ Prepare node ¶ Install Open-iSCSI: sudo pacman --noconfirm -Sy open-iscsi Enable and start iSCSI daemon: sudo systemctl enable --now iscsid Add disk to node ¶ Attach the disk Get device name: lsblk Double check it: sudo fdisk -l Wipe a partition ( /dev/sda1 ): sudo wipefs -fa /dev/sda1 Wipe a disk ( /dev/sda ): sudo wipefs -fa /dev/sda Create partition: sudo fdisk /dev/sda , n, p, , , , w Refresh disk information: sudo partprobe /dev/sda Format the disk: sudo mkfs.ext4 /dev/sda1 (replace /dev/sda1 by the name from step 2) Create mountpoint: sudo mkdir /mnt/lh01 (lh02, lh03, etc.) Mount: sudo mount /dev/sda1 /mnt/lh01 Get info to fstab line: cat /proc/self/mounts | grep /dev/sda1 Get disk UUID: lsblk -f Use info from steps 7 and 8 to create a permanent mount: sudo vim /etc/fstab # Longhorn storage UUID=825e9569-4086-4b24-a1ad-56a9b4a8fb4f /mnt/lh01 ext4 rw,relatime 0 0 Add disk to Longhorn ¶ Open Longhorn UI Node - burger menu at node's line - Edit node and disks - Add disk Troubleshoot ¶ Volume degraded ¶ Check logs of engine-manager pod on the node where there is volume failure Volume read-only ¶ Follow Troubleshooting: volume readonly or I/O error Restore from backup ¶ Follow Restoring Volumes for Kubernetes StatefulSets
docs.title: longhorn-system

docs.text: Install Open-iSCSI: sudo pacman --noconfirm -Sy open-iscsi Enable and start iSCSI daemon: sudo systemctl enable --now iscsid
docs.title: Prepare node

docs.text: Attach the disk Get device name: lsblk Double check it: sudo fdisk -l Wipe a partition ( /dev/sda1 ): sudo wipefs -fa /dev/sda1 Wipe a disk ( /dev/sda ): sudo wipefs -fa /dev/sda Create partition: sudo fdisk /dev/sda , n, p, , , , w Refresh disk information: sudo partprobe /dev/sda Format the disk: sudo mkfs.ext4 /dev/sda1 (replace /dev/sda1 by the name from step 2) Create mountpoint: sudo mkdir /mnt/lh01 (lh02, lh03, etc.) Mount: sudo mount /dev/sda1 /mnt/lh01 Get info to fstab line: cat /proc/self/mounts | grep /dev/sda1 Get disk UUID: lsblk -f Use info from steps 7 and 8 to create a permanent mount: sudo vim /etc/fstab # Longhorn storage UUID=825e9569-4086-4b24-a1ad-56a9b4a8fb4f /mnt/lh01 ext4 rw,relatime 0 0
docs.title: Add disk to node

docs.text: Open Longhorn UI Node - burger menu at node's line - Edit node and disks - Add disk

Qbittorrent ¶ Configuration ¶ Connect to WebUI using default credentials: admin - adminadmin Change the credentials in Options - Web UI - Authentication Tdarr ¶ Configuration ¶ Transcode options for a library: a. Re-order all streams V2: ProcessOrder: codecs,channels,languages,streamTypes channels: 7.1,5.1,2,1 streamTypes: video,audio,subtitle b. Migz-Remove image formats from file c. Migz-Clean audio streams: language: eng,und,ces,cze commentary: false tag_title: false d. Remove subtitles e. Migz-Transcode Using CPU & FFMPEG: container: mkv enable_10bit: false force_conform: false f. New file size check

docs.text: This hosts the applications to make my home smart. Naming conventions ¶ devices must have unique names device name must be meaningful group devices by rooms, purpose, zone Rooms ¶ Kids Bedroom Bedroom Kitchen Living Room Lights control ¶ <room> Light for the main light in the room: Bedroom Light <zone or location> <device type> for other lights: TV Area Lamp, Couch Strip, Island Center Light, Living Room Dimmer

docs.text: This hosts system upgrade automation. Prepare the nodes ¶ Label masters for k3s server upgrades: kubectl label nodes <MASTER_NODE> plan.upgrade.cattle.io/k3s-server=enabled Label workers for k3s agent upgrades: kubectl label nodes <WORKER_NODE> plan.upgrade.cattle.io/k3s-agent=enabled Label archlinux nodes (currently all of them): kubectl label nodes <NODE> plan.upgrade.cattle.io/archlinux=enabled You need to do that only once in node's lifetime Upgrade k3s ¶ Email is sent for releases watch on https://github.com/k3s-io/k3s Update version in system-upgrade/system-upgrade-controller/plans/k3s.yaml Apply the plan: kubectl apply -f system-upgrade/system-upgrade-controller/plans/k3s.yaml Upgrade system ¶ Update version in system-upgrade/system-upgrade-controller/plans/archlinux.yaml to today's date in yyyymmdd format Apply the plan: kubectl apply -f system-upgrade/system-upgrade-controller/plans/archlinux.yaml Get email with upgrade eligibility ¶ SSH to any node Install prerequisites: sudo pacman -S cronie s-nail Enable scheduling: sudo systemctl enable --now cronie Configure email: sudo vim /etc/mail.rc set v15-compat set from="B.U.V.I.S <postman@iron.buvis.net>" set mta=smtp://<user>:<password>@email-smtp.eu-west-1.amazonaws.com:587 \ smtp-use-starttls Create checker script: sudo vim /usr/local/bin/check-upgrade.sh from system-upgrade/system-upgrade-controller/check-upgrade.sh Make it executable: sudo chmod +x /usr/local/bin/check-upgrade.sh Schedule it: sudo crontab -e : # minute hour day_of_month month day_of_week command # | | | | | | 0 18 * * * /usr/local/bin/check-upgrade.sh Troubleshooting ¶ observe the upgrade: kubectl get all -n system-upgrade it seems deleting the jobs that are stuck is tolerated by the controller, but being patient (default poll interval is 15 minutes) is preferred
docs.title: system-upgrade

docs.text: Label masters for k3s server upgrades: kubectl label nodes <MASTER_NODE> plan.upgrade.cattle.io/k3s-server=enabled Label workers for k3s agent upgrades: kubectl label nodes <WORKER_NODE> plan.upgrade.cattle.io/k3s-agent=enabled Label archlinux nodes (currently all of them): kubectl label nodes <NODE> plan.upgrade.cattle.io/archlinux=enabled You need to do that only once in node's lifetime
docs.title: Prepare the nodes

docs.text: Email is sent for releases watch on https://github.com/k3s-io/k3s Update version in system-upgrade/system-upgrade-controller/plans/k3s.yaml Apply the plan: kubectl apply -f system-upgrade/system-upgrade-controller/plans/k3s.yaml

docs.title: Upgrade system

docs.text: SSH to any node Install prerequisites: sudo pacman -S cronie s-nail Enable scheduling: sudo systemctl enable --now cronie Configure email: sudo vim /etc/mail.rc set v15-compat set from="B.U.V.I.S <postman@iron.buvis.net>" set mta=smtp://<user>:<password>@email-smtp.eu-west-1.amazonaws.com:587 \ smtp-use-starttls Create checker script: sudo vim /usr/local/bin/check-upgrade.sh from system-upgrade/system-upgrade-controller/check-upgrade.sh Make it executable: sudo chmod +x /usr/local/bin/check-upgrade.sh Schedule it: sudo crontab -e : # minute hour day_of_month month day_of_week command # | | | | | | 0 18 * * * /usr/local/bin/check-upgrade.sh
docs.title: Get email with upgrade eligibility

docs.text: observe the upgrade: kubectl get all -n system-upgrade it seems deleting the jobs that are stuck is tolerated by the controller, but being patient (default poll interval is 15 minutes) is preferred
